engineering.translation: This verse is structurally unusual: Krishna stops arguing a position and instead names the architecture of his own teaching. What you have heard, he tells Arjuna, was sāṅkhya — analytical knowledge of what survives change. What follows is yoga — the disposition required to act in the light of that knowledge. He names two distinct intellectual disciplines and asserts both are required. In engineering, the senior practitioner holds two disciplines this verse authorizes naming separately. The first concerns architectural invariants and first-principles reasoning — distinguishing customer-need from implementation, spec from wire format, what survives a rewrite from what is the rewrite's clothing. The second concerns shipping discipline — the on-call doctrine, the deploy ethic, the iteration loop, decision under uncertainty. These are not two phases of one skill; they are distinct intellectual movements, and the senior engineer holding only one remains incomplete. The architect analyzing without shipping produces designs that never land in production. The shipper iterating without architectural reading produces rapid motion on the wrong substrate.
engineering.concrete_scenario: It is the end of a quarterly planning week, and the staff engineer is writing the team's narrative for the next quarter. They notice two distinct kinds of work need planning, and the team has been collapsing them into one. The first is architectural reading: what this product actually does, what the data flow looks like in five years if we are right, what is invariant under the next three product pivots, where the current implementation leaks abstractions. This shows up in design docs, RFCs, the architecture review channel. The second is shipping discipline: merge cadence, post-mortem quality, on-call rotation, rollback hygiene, canary deploys. This shows up in GitHub merge velocity, Datadog alerting health, Sentry error budgets. The staff engineer realizes the team has treated both as one budget, one calendar, one meeting cadence. Reading 2.39, they recognize the structural move: name the two disciplines explicitly, plan them separately, hold the team to both. The first quarter where both are named will look different from the previous four; this is what 'casting off the bondage of action' looks like operationally — work no longer collapses into one substrate where neither discipline is fully practiced.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader uses 'two disciplines' to justify treating one as optional. The verse names sāṅkhya AND yoga, both required, distinct, neither reducible to the other. An engineer who claims to be 'just a shipper' and refuses to develop architectural reading produces fast iteration on bad substrates; an engineer who claims to be 'just an architect' and refuses the shipping discipline produces unshipped designs. The verse authorizes the distinction; it does not authorize the abandonment of either side.
engineering.counter_example: The engineering analog preserves the META function of the verse but flattens one nuance the commentators preserve: the three traditions read 'yoge' differently (Shankara: karma-yoga as parallel discipline to jñāna; Ramanuja: yoga as the disposition for acting in the light of self-knowledge, sequential not parallel; Vishvanatha: yoga as bhakti-yoga). The engineering layer adopts the Shankara reading (parallel disciplines) because it is the most operationally useful for the engineer. A reader from the Vishishtadvaita or Gaudiya tradition will rightly note that the engineering reading does not preserve their reading's specific structural claim about precedence or about devotion. The engineering layer is honest about which of the three commentarial readings it is anchored to, and does not claim to capture all three.
engineering.implication: Name the two disciplines on your team explicitly: the architectural reading and the shipping discipline. Plan calendar, headcount, and review forums for each separately. The team where both are named and practiced is the team where action stops producing more action without reducing the underlying problem.
engineering.quotable_line: Architectural reading and shipping discipline are two skills, not one — and the senior engineer holds both, named and practiced separately.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 2.39 is the META structural pivot — Krishna explicitly names sāṅkhya AND yoga as the two doctrines and pivots from one to the other. This is the verse that AUTHORIZES the chapter thesis's three-thread reading. Engineering layer honors the META function: the engineering analog names the senior engineer's two distinct disciplines (architectural invariants / first-principles AND shipping discipline / iteration loop) and authorizes operational separation. CRITICAL care: the engineering layer adopts Shankara's reading (parallel disciplines, both required) and is honest in the counter-example about not capturing Ramanuja's sequential reading or Vishvanatha's bhakti reading. The 'karma-bandham prahāsyasi' promise is rendered as 'work no longer collapses into one substrate where neither discipline is fully practiced.' Concrete scenario uses quarterly planning with explicit references to design docs, RFCs, architecture review, post-mortems, on-call, rollback, canary, GitHub, Datadog, Sentry. Tags: first-principles, shipping-discipline, architectural-invariant.

v1: trimmed translation from 250 to under 180 words by collapsing the doctrinal-prologue into a tighter exposition (kept the META naming, kept both disciplines, removed redundant restatement); concrete_scenario tightened from 235 to ~205 words; traditional_meaning_consensus in source pack tightened from 216 to ~175 words to bring 11.1 sum under 600; implication tightened. Lexical diversity lifted to ≥0.55 by varying repeated terms ('discipline' / 'movement' / 'practice'; 'engineer' / 'practitioner' / 'team').
